Some say bandits, some say the mines ran dry. Either way, stock up.
The traders won't talk about it. That alone tells you something.
Prices like these don't happen without a reason.
Last caravan that came through had half its wagons empty. Driver said he couldn't find sellers.
Iron's worth double what it was a month ago. Whoever's hoarding it has deep pockets.
The merchant guild rerouted everything through the northern pass. Longer road, higher cost, fewer shipments.
I've been doing this long enough to know when someone's buying up stock to corner a market.
There's good coin to be made running supplies east right now. If you don't mind the risk.
The dye merchants packed up and left two weeks ago. Didn't sell their remaining stock, just left.
Grain prices are steady here, but one settlement over they're paying triple. Something's cutting off their supply.
A buyer came through offering gold for medicinal herbs. Whole cartloads. Wouldn't say who they were for.
Nobody's seen a shipment of timber in weeks. The mill upstream is either closed or selling to someone else.
You can tell a lot from what people stop buying. Nobody's buying seed grain. They don't expect to plant.
The smiths are melting down old tools for the metal. That's not frugality, that's desperation.
Word is the main routes are closed. Salt and fish will get expensive soon.
Someone's paying mercenaries to escort wagons on a road that used to be perfectly safe.
Every time a new trader shows up, they're charging more and carrying less. The math isn't hard.
The jeweler left town overnight. Took everything. When they run, the trouble's already close.
Leather's cheap because the herds are being culled. People are scared to keep livestock out in the open.
Barter's coming back. When people stop trusting coin, that tells you how they feel about the future.
Flour quality has dropped. They're cutting it with something. You can taste the difference if you know what to look for.
The ferry operator raised his prices without warning. Says the risk has gone up. Won't say what the risk is.
Three caravans diverted in one month. They're not taking the southern road anymore and nobody is explaining why.
Copper's scarce. Not because the mines are empty: because the miners won't go deep enough anymore.
The weavers can't get good wool. What arrives is coarse, brittle, from animals that haven't been fed properly.
We used to get shipments on a schedule. Now they arrive when they arrive, if they arrive.
A merchant tried to pay in foreign coin that nobody recognized. He seemed surprised we wouldn't take it.
The chandler is rationing candles. Tallow is in short supply and the rendered fat that's available has gone rancid.
Charcoal prices have tripled since the forest road closed. The smiths are furious.
A trader tried to sell spoiled grain at full price. Bold move. The market master threw him out.
The wine from the southern vintners tastes wrong this year. Thinner, more acidic. Bad harvest or bad handling.
Nobody's restocking rope. That seems like a small thing until you think about everything rope is used for.
We're seeing goods marked with stamps from settlements that don't normally trade with us. Supply lines are shifting.
The glassware merchant left a standing order and never came back to fill it. Paid in advance and vanished.
Spices that used to be affordable are now a luxury. The eastern routes must be compromised.
The lumber we're getting now is green. Unseasoned, still heavy with sap. Whoever's selling it is cutting faster than they can dry it.
Clay for pottery is coming from a different source. It fires to a different color. The potters can work with it, but they're not happy.
The tanners are working overtime but producing less. Something is wrong with the hides they're receiving: thin, scarred, poorly processed.
A merchant guild representative visited last week. Took notes, asked about storage capacity, and left without buying anything. That's reconnaissance, not commerce.
Prices are stable, which is the problem. They should be dropping after harvest. If they're holding steady, someone is warehousing supply.
Tin has disappeared from the market entirely. Bronze production has stalled. Anyone with existing bronze stock is sitting on a fortune.
The soap makers can't get lye. They're buying ash from anyone who has it, but ash supplies depend on woodcutters, and the woodcutters are short-handed.
A warehouse burned. Official cause is accident, but the timing was convenient for certain merchants who had competing stock.
The road tolls went up again. Third time this season. The justification is road maintenance, but the roads look the same.
Honey is worth more than butter now. The beekeepers say the colonies are stressed, but they won't say what's stressing them.
Someone dumped a load of counterfeit silver into the market. By the time anyone noticed, it had circulated widely. Trust is damaged.
The fishmongers have nothing but river catch. The coastal fish stopped coming in three weeks ago.
I watched a trader haggle for an hour over a price he would have accepted without question last season. Everyone is squeezing harder.
The healers are buying up every scrap of certain herbs. Panic buying or advance warning: either way, the rest of us can't get any.
Salt has become currency in practice. People trade it directly rather than converting to coin first.
A new tariff on imports hit without warning. The merchants are passing the cost straight to us.
The farrier is booked out for weeks. Not enough iron for horseshoes. Animals are going unshod.
A blight hit the flax crop. Linen won't be available in quantity until next year at the earliest.
Exotic goods have stopped appearing entirely. Whatever connections supplied them are severed.
The market is smaller every week. Fewer stalls, fewer goods, fewer buyers. The square feels empty.
A consortium bought out the entire grain reserve. They're releasing it slowly, at a markup. Legal, but barely.
The carriers are demanding hazard pay. Something on the road spooked them and now every delivery costs more.
Stone for building is unavailable. The quarry workers walked off over a wage dispute and nobody has replaced them.
Fresh fruit hasn't appeared at market in weeks. The orchards to the south aren't shipping, and nobody will say why.
The mason found cracks in the storage cellar. Water's getting in. We'll lose stock if it isn't fixed, but fixing it requires materials we can't get.
Axle grease is in short supply. Sounds trivial until the wagon wheels start seizing up and nothing moves.
The butcher is selling cuts he never would have offered before. Scraps repackaged as proper meat. He's stretching what he has.
Pigments for dyes are being hoarded by the guild. Independents can't get color at any price.
A fleet of merchant barges was spotted heading upstream, fully loaded. None of them stopped here. They're supplying someone else.
The price of nails has increased sevenfold. Every building project in the settlement is stalled.
Tea and coffee are luxuries now. Whatever disrupted the long-distance routes hit the stimulant trade hardest.
A trade delegation arrived, surveyed the market, and left without making a single purchase. They were assessing us, not buying.
The coopers can't keep up with demand. Everyone wants barrels for storage, which means everyone is stockpiling.
Horse feed is more expensive than bread. The stables are considering reducing the herd just to cut costs.
A major buyer defaulted on a contract. The ripple effect is still being felt: suppliers who counted on that payment are now defaulting on their own obligations.
Sulfur is being bought in quantity by someone outside the settlement. The miners are happy to sell, but the buyers won't identify themselves.
The potters have switched to cheaper clay. The resulting product is functional but ugly. Nobody's complaining because at least it's available.
An entire shipment of cured meat arrived spoiled. The supplier blamed the journey. The carrier blamed the supplier. We're out the cost either way.
Thread is expensive because the spinners' guild raised their rates. The weavers can't absorb the cost and are passing it forward.
The apothecary's shelves are half empty. Not for lack of business: for lack of ingredients.
Tin cups and plates are replacing pottery in the market. People want durability over beauty when times are uncertain.
The docks are quieter than I've ever seen them. Half the berths are empty. The boats that are here aren't unloading much.
A merchant offered to sell weapons at a discount. When demand for weapons rises and the price drops, someone is flooding the market deliberately.
Seed stock for next season is being bought up early. Farmers are nervous and the smart ones are securing what they need now.
The smithy is running on half shifts. Not by choice: the coal supply only sustains limited hours of forging.
Alum is unavailable. Without it, the leather workers can't properly tan and the dyers can't set color. Two trades affected by one shortage.
A trader set up shop and undercut every price in the market by a quarter. Lasted three days, cleaned out, and left. He was buying information as much as selling goods.
The tavern switched from imported ale to local brew. The quality drop was noticed. The reason was economic, not patriotic.
Parchment is being rationed. The scribe says one more season like this and records will have to be kept on slate.
We had a surplus of pickled vegetables last season. This season, the same stock would sell at five times the price.
Quicksilver appeared on the market for the first time in years. Nobody local has a use for it. Someone's buying it for specialized work.
The caravansary is charging double for stabling. They know the traders have no alternative and they're leveraging it.
The guild revoked a local merchant's license. The official reason was overdue fees. The real reason is she was undercutting guild prices.
Firewood is being sold by weight instead of volume. A small change that adds up to paying more for the same heat.
The herbal remedy that kept the fever down last winter is no longer available at any price. The plant it's made from doesn't grow here.
A wagon of ceramic goods arrived shattered. Not from road damage: the packing was fine. The goods were deliberately smashed before shipment.
The fishermen are keeping more of their catch for personal use. What reaches the market is smaller, older stock.
An embargo, unofficial but real, is keeping goods from crossing a particular boundary. Nobody will say who ordered it.
Glass is worth more than metal here now. Windows are a luxury. The glassblowers have a waiting list that stretches to next year.
Goose down and feathers are commanding absurd prices. Every household that can pluck is plucking. Bedding has become an investment.
The courier system has broken down. Messages are arriving weeks late, which means orders are placed on outdated information.
Fuel for the kilns is scarce. The brick makers are burning anything they can find: furniture, deadfall, even dried dung.
Contracts are being written with penalty clauses that didn't exist a year ago. Nobody trusts a handshake anymore.
The money changers are taking a larger cut. They say the risk of counterfeit justifies it. Maybe it does.
The saddler can't get proper leather and is using an inferior substitute. The saddles look fine but they won't last a season.
Old debts are being called in. People who owed favors or goods are being pressed to deliver now, regardless of circumstances.
The stoneware from the northern kilns used to be cheap and abundant. Now it arrives in small lots at premium prices.
A new trade route opened to the west. The merchants who invested early are doing well. Everyone else is playing catch-up.
Lamp oil is rationed to one flask per household per week. After dark, most homes sit in the glow of a single candle.
The baker is using a different grain. It tastes fine, but the bread is denser and doesn't keep as long. He won't say where it comes from.
Wax is expensive because the chandlers and the sealers are competing for the same supply. Documents go unsealed or candles go unlit.
A speculator bought the entire hazelnut harvest. Reselling at triple. Nobody can afford to bake properly.
The linen merchants are selling undyed cloth only. Dyed fabric requires mordants they can no longer source.
Vinegar production has stopped. The acetic culture died and the replacement hasn't arrived. Preservation relies on salt alone now.
The trade fair was canceled. First time in living memory. The organizers cited insufficient participation, but the real issue is safety on the roads.
A traveling tinker offered to repair metal goods for food instead of coin. That's not generosity: that's a man who can't buy a meal.
Cattle are being slaughtered younger because the feed can't sustain them to full weight. The meat is lean and the hides are small.
The sawmill's output has dropped by half. Not equipment failure: the workers are being pulled away to other settlements that pay more.
We're importing what we used to export. That reversal happened inside of two months.
The market inspectors are more aggressive than usual. Fines for short weight, improper labeling, expired goods. Revenue generation disguised as regulation.
Moss is being harvested as packing material because proper straw is going to the stables. Every shortage cascades into another.
A forge went cold for the first time since it was built. The smith says he'll relight it when he can afford fuel. Nobody is sure when that will be.
The demand for preserving salt has outstripped supply. People are drying and smoking everything instead. The smokehouses run day and night.
Tools that would normally be replaced are being repaired repeatedly. The cost of new metal exceeds the cost of a smith's time, for now.
The wheelwright has a six-week backlog. Every farmer with a cracked axle is waiting, and waiting means the harvest waits too.
A merchant was caught watering the wine. Not a scandal in itself, but the fact that anyone noticed means people are paying closer attention to what they're getting.
Hide glue is substituting for better adhesives. The furniture holds, but it won't survive a wet season.
The brewery cut its output to stretch the malt supply. Rationing hasn't started officially, but the smaller kegs tell the story.
Bone meal for the fields is expensive now that the slaughterhouses are running at reduced capacity. Less meat means less waste means less fertilizer.
A secondhand goods market has sprung up. People selling things they'd normally keep. That's the clearest sign of how tight things are.
Canvas is worth its weight because the sail makers and the tent makers are bidding against each other for the same bolts.
A contract for road repair was awarded and then revoked when the materials budget doubled. The road stays broken.
Dried fish has replaced fresh in most households. Not by preference: by availability.
The beeswax supply is controlled by a single apiary. They know it and price accordingly.
Lime for mortar is available but the transport cost has made building prohibitively expensive.
Someone's been stealing from the communal root cellar. Small amounts, consistently. The shortage is cumulative.
The cloth dyers are mixing colors to stretch their pigment. Greens are muddier, reds are duller. Nobody's ordering new banners.
Mead production has ceased entirely. Honey goes to the apothecary now, not the brewer.
A surplus of one material can't compensate for the lack of another. We have more clay than we can use and not enough fuel to fire it.
The tax collectors arrived a month early this year. The coffers were not ready. Interest accrues on the shortfall.
Flint and steel kits are selling briskly. People are hedging against losing access to the communal fire pit.
The tallow supply depends on the slaughterhouses, which depend on the feed supply, which depends on the harvest. One failure cascades through everything.
A merchant from the east offered rare spices in exchange for information about local defenses. She was turned away. The spices were genuine.
The price of rope has doubled because the hemp fields were repurposed for food crops. Practical, but now the fishers can't repair their nets.
Every empty storefront on the market row tells a story. That's four more this season than last.
Night soil collectors are charging more. Nobody thinks about sanitation until the price goes up.
The arrowsmith is making more arrowheads than usual. Somebody is buying in bulk and it isn't the guard.
Scrap metal is being collected door to door. Someone's paying for it and the amount they're offering suggests they need it badly.
The trade ledger shows a deficit for five consecutive months. We're spending more than we're bringing in and the reserves won't last forever.
A peddler sold miracle cures outside the market. Half the settlement bought some before the healer examined it and found it was colored water.
The blacksmiths formed a pricing agreement. Nobody calls it a cartel, but the effect is the same.
Surplus wool was sent downriver. The council needed the coin more than the weavers needed the fiber. Short-term thinking.
Imported steel is of noticeably lower quality this season. Either the source has changed or someone is substituting cheaper stock.
The last traveling merchant said she won't be back this way for at least a season. The route isn't worth the risk.
Hops are being grown where barley used to be. More profitable per acre, but it means we import the grain we used to grow.
Every trader who passes through now carries at least one armed guard. The cost gets added to every item they sell.
The smelters are running short on flux. Without it, the ore produces inferior metal. The whole chain weakens from one missing ingredient.
An agreement between two settlements to share surplus collapsed when one side decided their surplus was no longer surplus.
The furrier has pelts but no buyers. Luxury goods are the first thing people stop spending on.
Turpentine is unavailable. The painters and the wood sealers are competing for any resinous substitute they can find.
Pack animals are being requisitioned. The settlement needs transport more than private traders do, so the traders walk.
A cargo of good steel sat on the dock for three days because nobody could agree on the import duty. By the time it was resolved, the buyer had gone elsewhere.
The road maintenance fund is empty. Potholes are expanding. The merchants adjust their prices to cover the wagon damage.
Every surplus we produce gets claimed before it reaches open market. Standing contracts absorb it all. New buyers are out of luck.
The shipwrights have more work than they can handle. Everyone wants river vessels. The demand for overland transport is being abandoned.
Seeds are being traded like currency. Specific varieties, verified strain, sealed containers. The smart farmers saw this coming.
A tax on stored goods was introduced. The immediate effect: everyone moved stock and nobody declared accurate inventories.
We produce enough food but not enough variety. Nutrition suffers when the only thing available is grain and root vegetables.
The journeyman system is failing. Apprentices can't travel because the roads aren't safe. Skills stop spreading.
A bulk order of medicinal supplies was diverted by the regional authority. We paid for it. They took it. No refund, no explanation.
Locally made goods are improving in quality. When imports dry up, people invest in what they can produce themselves.
The communal stores are at their lowest point since the settlement was founded. The stockmaster checks the levels every morning and the number only goes down.
A pair of oxen sold for what a full team would have cost last year. Draft animals are scarce because nobody is breeding when feed is this expensive.
The glazier left to work for a wealthier settlement. We cannot replace him and every cracked pane stays cracked.
Wool is being traded raw because the settlement cannot afford to process it. The value leaves with the bale and comes back as finished cloth at triple the price.
The herbalist started growing her own stock rather than buying it. She says the quality is lower but at least it is reliable. Independence costs something.
A merchant was found carrying two sets of scales: one for buying and one for selling. The weights differed by just enough to profit on both ends.
Chalk for marking timber is gone. Sounds absurd, but without it the sawyer cannot work accurately and every board is a gamble.
The cooper's hoops are iron, and iron is expensive. Every barrel now costs what two did last season. Storage becomes a luxury.
Amber resin appeared at market for the first time. Nobody knows the source and the seller was evasive, but the quality was undeniable.
A group of farmers pooled their resources to buy seed directly from the source, cutting out the merchant. The merchant retaliated by refusing to carry their harvest.
The last delivery of finished cloth was moth-eaten. Not visibly: the damage only showed when the fabric was stretched. A total loss disguised as a full shipment.
Paper is being made locally from linen rags because the imported pulp stopped coming. The result is coarse and uneven, but it takes ink.
The stockyard is empty more days than it is full. Traders who used to stay a week now pass through in a day because there is nothing worth lingering for.
Pitch for waterproofing is available only in small quantities. The roofers and the boatbuilders are bidding against each other and the price benefits neither.
A merchant brought an entirely new type of grain from a distant region. It grows faster than what we plant but nobody knows if the soil here will sustain it.
The tallow shortage has forced the chandler to thin her candles. They burn a third faster and cost the same. The math is not lost on anyone.
Good steel is being recycled from old horseshoes. The smith says it is honest metal, better than the new stock arriving from the east. That tells you everything about current supply quality.
Dried herbs are selling at fresh-herb prices because the supply chain now includes an extra middleman who contributes nothing but cost.
The cider press broke and the replacement parts must come from a foundry two settlements away. The apples are rotting while the paperwork clears.
A caravan master offered to carry our goods for free on condition that we grant his company exclusive trading rights for a year. The council is tempted. They should not be.
The lye ash surplus from the winter hearths was bought up by a single buyer before the soap makers could get to it. Whoever it was knew the schedule.
Block salt is being chipped and sold in fragments rather than whole blocks. A sign that the importers are maximizing revenue from a shrinking supply.
Beeswax candles have become a status symbol. Tallow is for the rest of us. The division is new and unwelcome.
A weaver offered to take payment in raw fiber rather than coin. That arrangement benefits both parties and cuts the merchant entirely out of the chain.
The demand for baskets has tripled. Everyone needs portable storage now that the communal warehouse is full. The basket makers cannot keep up.
Lamp black for ink has become scarce, which means records are being kept in diluted batches. The scribe says half his entries will be illegible within five years.
Dried fruit that used to fill a market stall now fits in a single crate. The orchards are producing, but the drying sheds were repurposed for grain storage.
The hide merchant claims his prices reflect the quality of the stock. The cobblers who buy from him say the hides are thinner than last year and the prices are higher. Both statements are true.
Niter for preservation is controlled by a guild that sets allocations. If your household is not on the list, you do not get any. The list has not been updated in three years.
Someone introduced a barter board at the market: a public posting where goods are offered and sought without coin changing hands. The merchants hate it. Everyone else finds it useful.
The disruption near {subject_focus_the} is the root cause. Everything downstream of that route is affected.
Goods that used to come through {subject_focus_the} are being rerouted. Longer transit, higher cost.
The merchants who depended on {subject_focus_the} are scrambling for alternatives. None are as efficient.
Whoever controls access to {subject_focus_the} right now has leverage over every trader in the area.
The warehouse near {subject_focus_the} has been inaccessible for weeks. Inventory is sitting there spoiling.
Supply chains that relied on {subject_focus_the} are broken. Rebuilding them will take time and coin.
The market reacted to the news about {subject_focus_the} within hours. Prices adjusted before the council even met.
Materials that used to be cheap because of {subject_focus_the} are now premium goods. The economy has shifted.
A consortium tried to force access to {subject_focus_the} and lost a wagon in the process.
The tax revenue from {subject_focus_the} was funding half the settlement's operations. That income has stopped.
Merchants are treating goods from near {subject_focus_the} as suspect. Quality concerns, they say.
The road repair budget for the route to {subject_focus_the} has been reallocated. The council is cutting losses.
A speculator bought up contracts tied to {subject_focus_the} for pennies. If the route reopens, they profit enormously.
The ferry service that connected to {subject_focus_the} is operating at a loss and may shut down entirely.
Artisans who sourced rare materials from {subject_focus_the} are switching to inferior substitutes.
A trade envoy was sent to negotiate passage near {subject_focus_the}. They have not reported back yet.
The price instability caused by {subject_focus_the} is spreading to markets that have no direct connection.
Caravans are adding armed escorts specifically for the stretch near {subject_focus_the}. That cost gets passed on.
The seasonal trade fair that depended on goods from {subject_focus_the} may be canceled for the first time.
A guild master said the problems at {subject_focus_the} have set back trade agreements by a full year.
Stockpiles built before the trouble at {subject_focus_the} are running low. Another month and they will be gone.
The insurance premiums for shipments near {subject_focus_the} have tripled. Some carriers cannot afford it.
A black market has sprung up for goods allegedly salvaged from {subject_focus_the}. Authenticity is questionable.
The loss of {subject_focus_the} as a trade node has created a bottleneck that affects the entire region.
A factor calculated the economic impact of losing {subject_focus_the}. The number was large enough to get the council's attention.
